patterns.title: Oversight Mode Selection
patterns.summary: Stop treating human oversight as a binary. Match the oversight mode to the decision, the time horizon, and the reversibility of harm.
whenToUse: A client says every AI decision must be human-reviewed
A board wants 'human in the loop' but cannot define what that means operationally
An agentic use case mixes low-risk routine actions with high-risk escalations
howToRun: Map the workflow into decision points, not systems.
Separate constitutive gates from corrective interventions.
Define where anticipatory oversight is needed because real-time review is infeasible.
Write explicit trigger conditions for escalation, pause, and override.
clientLines: The question isn't whether a human is in the loop. It's what kind of loop, where, and with what actual intervention power.
If the human can only watch after the fact, call that monitoring, not control.
watchouts: Rubber-stamp review passed off as meaningful control
Kill-switch theatre without genuine understanding or traceability
Designing oversight around org charts instead of workflow reality

patterns.title: Trust Provenance Ladder
patterns.summary: Trace trust below the model. Ask what is grounded in reality, what is proxy, what is inherited, and what degrades in production.
whenToUse: A client wants assurance over an AI system but starts at model outputs
A model vendor claims strong trustworthiness without deep evidence on data or monitoring
You need to explain why 'accurate in testing' is not enough
howToRun: Start at output claims and walk backwards: output, deployment, model, training process, labels, source reality.
Mark each layer as observed, inferred, proxied, or assumed.
Identify which properties can be certified once versus continuously monitored.
Highlight where institutional judgement entered the chain.
clientLines: Most AI trust claims are downstream claims built on upstream assumptions.
Ground truth is usually assembled, not discovered. That's where assurance has to begin.
watchouts: Treating benchmark accuracy as a substitute for provenance
Ignoring label quality and institutional assumptions
Assuming static trust properties survive deployment unchanged

patterns.title: Path-Dependent Control Design
patterns.summary: Evaluate risky sequences, not just single actions. Many agent failures only become visible across a chain of individually acceptable steps.
whenToUse: A client is deploying copilots or agents with tools, APIs, or system access
Control owners focus on prompts, permissions, or individual tool calls
You suspect that the real risk sits in action combinations rather than isolated moves
howToRun: List the top 5-10 mission paths the agent can execute.
Identify sequence-level failure modes: exfiltration, privilege escalation, hidden state changes, policy evasion.
Define runtime policies on paths, not just requests.
Add immutable logging at the path and handoff layer.
clientLines: A safe action followed by another safe action can still create an unsafe path.
Prompt controls are not governance if the violation only appears at the sequence level.
watchouts: Over-reliance on prompt wording
Approval gates that check single actions in isolation
No reconstruction of multi-step execution paths

patterns.title: Moral Crumple Zone Test
patterns.summary: Check whether the human is actually improving safety, or simply absorbing blame after the fact.
whenToUse: A client mandates human approval for high-risk AI outputs
An executive says 'a human signs off, so we're covered'
You suspect the review task exceeds realistic human cognitive capacity
howToRun: Define exactly what the human reviewer must detect, verify, and challenge.
Test whether a competent reviewer can do that within the available time and evidence.
If not, redesign the system so the human governs framing, thresholds, and escalation instead of pretending to verify every output.
Document where accountability truly sits: design, deployment, monitoring, or review.
clientLines: If the human cannot realistically detect the failure, you've built a liability absorber, not a control.
Presence is not oversight. Sign-off is not safety.
watchouts: Compliance theatre disguised as oversight
Humans asked to verify opaque outputs at machine speed
Blame shifted downward while design decisions remain unscrutinised

patterns.title: Third-Party AI Materiality Review
patterns.summary: Treat major AI vendors like material service providers when they sit inside critical workflows. Most organisations are under-governing them.
whenToUse: A bank or insurer uses OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure AI, Bedrock, or Vertex in material processes
The procurement or legal team treats the vendor as ordinary SaaS
A client needs a CPS 230-style discussion without hand-waving
howToRun: Map which business services actually depend on the AI vendor.
Assess concentration, substitutability, audit rights, subcontractor opacity, and incident notification obligations.
Separate model risk from service dependency risk.
Force a decision: tolerate gap, negotiate controls, or redesign dependence.
clientLines: If the service is material, the AI vendor is not 'just software'. It's part of your operating resilience perimeter.
The hidden risk is not just model quality. It's contractual weakness plus dependency depth.
watchouts: AI procurement done outside resilience or third-party risk frameworks
No practical fallback if the vendor changes terms or fails
Assuming hyperscaler branding equals adequate control evidence

patterns.title: Committee Diffusion Breaker
patterns.summary: When everyone touches AI governance, nobody owns it. Force named decisions, named artifacts, and named review triggers.
whenToUse: A client has multiple committees and no one can answer who owns what
AI risk, cyber, legal, model risk, procurement, and business all assume someone else has checked it
You need to cut through governance sprawl fast
howToRun: For each major decision, assign one decision owner, one challenge owner, and one evidence owner.
Require a concrete artifact per control claim: test result, review note, policy exception, or log evidence.
Define escalation triggers and expiry dates for temporary tolerances.
Remove committee language until accountability is legible.
clientLines: A committee can review a decision, but it cannot own one in the forensic sense regulators care about.
If you can't point to the artifact and the accountable owner, you don't have governance. You have atmosphere.
watchouts: Shared accountability becoming no accountability
Control claims with no attached evidence object
Temporary workarounds becoming permanent invisible policy

patterns.title: Assurance Stack by Lifecycle Stage
patterns.summary: Split assurance into pre-deployment, deployment, and post-deployment layers. Different trust claims belong at different stages.
whenToUse: A client asks for 'an AI assurance framework'
Internal audit wants a coherent review structure
A vendor lumps design evidence and runtime evidence together
howToRun: List the trust claims being made: accuracy, fairness, robustness, provenance, overrideability, incident response.
Assign each claim to the stage where it can actually be evidenced.
Make explicit which claims decay and need continuous evidence.
Design assurance reports that distinguish inherited evidence from live evidence.
clientLines: You cannot audit runtime drift with design documents, and you cannot prove training integrity with production dashboards.
Assurance gets stronger when claims are matched to the stage where they can genuinely be evidenced.
watchouts: One-off validation presented as ongoing assurance
Monitoring controls with no tie back to original trust claims
Audit scopes that blur design and runtime evidence

patterns.title: Consultation Reframe: From AI Risk to Decision Rights
patterns.summary: When a conversation gets abstract, reframe it around who is allowed to decide what, with what evidence, and what happens when they are wrong.
whenToUse: A client meeting drifts into generic AI ethics language
Senior executives want something practical and immediate
You need to bridge trust, governance, and liability in one move
howToRun: Ask what decisions the AI is influencing, recommending, or making.
For each decision, ask who currently owns it, who can intervene, and what evidence they see.
Then ask what artifact would prove this was governed well after an incident.
Use the answers to redesign controls around real decision rights rather than abstract principles.
clientLines: Trust is too vague to govern directly. Decision rights are not.
The fastest way to make this practical is to ask: who decides, on what basis, and who can stop it?
watchouts: Abstract principle statements with no operational anchor
Confusing recommendation systems with non-decision systems
No forensic trail for why a decision was allowed to stand

patterns.title: Know Your Agent (KYA) Review
patterns.summary: Use KYA to make agent systems legible. Trace identity, lineage, authority, runtime dependencies, evidence, and accountable ownership before an agent is allowed into material workflows.
whenToUse: A client is buying or piloting an external agentic AI product
A vendor demo looks impressive but accountability is opaque
An institution needs a practical bridge between third-party risk, model risk, and operational governance
howToRun: Identify the agent and require a persistent attributable identity, not just a product name.
Map lineage: model provider, orchestration layer, wrappers, tool connectors, and subcontractors.
Document authority: what the agent can read, write, trigger, approve, or escalate.
Inspect runtime evidence: logs, traces, tool-call records, provenance artifacts, and shutdown mechanisms.
Assign one accountable owner internally and one named control owner for exceptions, reviews, and incidents.
clientLines: If you cannot identify the agent, you cannot meaningfully govern it.
KYA is the operational layer between knowing your vendor and trusting your workflow.
watchouts: Questionnaire theatre with no runtime verification
Hidden subcontractors or wrapper layers
No persistent logs of tool use or delegated actions
Confusing vendor reputation with agent controllability
